Project Documentation

TPM Take-Home Assessment

I built an end-to-end automation of UAT defect management — from setting up Jira and Google Drive integrations to two production-ready AI skills: /defects-update, which syncs vendor defect sheets into Jira idempotently, and /dashboard-comms, which generates a live defect dashboard and drafts tailored stakeholder communications — all powered by Claude Code and the Model Context Protocol.

Completed June 2026
Jira · Google Drive · Claude Code · GitHub
Vidya Unni
Objective & Workflow
⚡ The Problem
1
QA and testing is outsourced to a vendor who operates independently of the organisation's toolchain. The vendor tracks and manages defects in their own their own internal tooling, sharing progress via versioned Excel exports attached to status emails — with no direct access to or integration with our in-house Jira.
2
Each defect update cycle required the TPM to manually extract defect data from email attachments, reconcile it against the existing Jira backlog, create or update Bug records, map each defect to its impacted user story, and apply the correct workflow status — a repetitive, error-prone process that consumed significant daily effort and introduced lag between the vendor's QA register and the organisation's project view.
3
With defect data siloed in the vendor's system and Jira perpetually out of sync, generating timely, accurate stakeholder communications — whether a sprint health summary for leadership, a technical briefing for engineering, or a confidence update for the customer — required additional manual effort to aggregate, interpret, and format, compounding the administrative overhead with every release cycle.

I selected this problem statement from my current organisation because it represents one of the most common yet underestimated sources of TPM overhead — the manual effort of maintaining a live, accurate project status view when core execution sits with an external vendor operating in a separate toolchain.

This project is my attempt to demonstrate how AI-augmented workflow automation — specifically through agentic orchestration via the Model Context Protocol (MCP) — can serve as a force multiplier for programme management. By deploying a large language model as the reasoning layer between the vendor's defect register and our in-house Jira, I was able to eliminate the daily reconciliation loop that previously required manual data extraction, cross-referencing, and re-entry.

The result is a shift from reactive, human-mediated data synchronisation to an on-demand, AI-driven pipeline that maintains bidirectional traceability between defects and user stories, applies intelligent field mapping and status transitions, and surfaces blockers in real time. This directly reduces operational toil, improves the fidelity of Jira as a single source of truth, and compresses the time-to-insight for stakeholder communications — from daily manual effort to a single skill invocation.

✦ The Solution

End-to-end automation of daily defect updates from the vendor's Excel sheet into our Jira — creating new bugs, updating existing ones with the latest information, and linking every defect to its impacted user story. This eliminates the manual reconciliation loop and ensures Jira always reflects the current state of the vendor's defect register without TPM intervention.

→ Workflow Built So Far
Prototype Framework Base integrations that establish the environment and seed data for the AI skills to operate against
Jira MCP Connected
Claude Code connected to my personal Jira (SCRUM project), seeded with sample data, via the Atlassian MCP server, enabling programmatic read and write access to all issue types.
User Stories Created from Requirements
17 user stories created in Jira from requirement snapshots, each labelled with a GSMR key to serve as the traceability anchor for downstream defect linking.
Google Drive Auth Established
OAuth2 credentials configured via @modelcontextprotocol/server-gdrive to authenticate against the "Transformation Project" Google Drive folder. The MCP server is used solely for authentication — the sheet itself is read programmatically by read_defects.mjs using the googleapis Drive API to export it as CSV.
↓ skills built on top of this foundation
AI Solutions Agentic Claude Code skills that deliver the actual automation — these are the production-ready outputs
/defects-update
Skill
Reads the vendor's latest defect sheet from Google Drive, maps each defect to its parent user story via GSMR label, and idempotently creates or updates Jira Bugs — with status transitions, priority mapping, and story links — on demand. Eliminates the daily manual reconciliation loop entirely.
---
name: defects-update
description: Sync the UAT defect list from the "Transformation Project" Google Drive folder into the Transformation Project (SCRUM) Jira project as Bugs — mapping each defect to its user story by label, resolving ambiguous rows with the user, and creating or updating Bugs idempotently with no duplicates. Invoke with /defects-update.
user-invocable: true
---

# Defects Update

Sync UAT defects (a versioned Google Sheet) into Jira as Bugs under the right user stories,
idempotently. Follow the steps **in order**. Do not skip the confirmation and verification gates.

## Fixed facts about this environment

- **Jira site / cloudId:** `vidhyaunni6.atlassian.net` → `3215a260-ee4c-42fd-8255-0c4dec1fb617`
- **Jira project "Transformation Project" = key `SCRUM`** (team-managed). Bug issuetype id `10007`, Story id `10004`.
- **Drive folder "Transformation Project"** id `1-uTFK62qH0fTkvx3HLWgLYP2DBvTW1y-` — **the only in-scope source.**
- **Story↔label rule:** every SCRUM Story carries a label equal to its GSMR key. Build this map live in Step 3
  (do not hardcode). Static fallback: `jira-key-mapping.csv` in the project root.
- **Helper script:** `scripts/read_defects.mjs` (Drive-only; Jira is done by you via the `atlassian` MCP tools).

In every `atlassian` tool call pass `cloudId: "3215a260-ee4c-42fd-8255-0c4dec1fb617"`.

---

## Step 1 — Locate the latest version (folder-scoped) and confirm

Run from the project root:

```bash
node .claude/skills/defects-update/scripts/read_defects.mjs versions
```

This returns the defect-list files in the Transformation Project folder, newest-first (by version, then
modified time). Scope is **strictly this folder** — do not search elsewhere in Drive.

- Take `files[0]` as the candidate latest. Tell the user its `name`, `version`, and `modifiedTime`.
- **If more than one version exists**, explicitly say which is newer and **ask the user to confirm it is indeed
  the latest** before continuing.
- If only one exists, still confirm it with the user.
- If `count` is 0, stop and report that no matching file was found in the folder.

Do not proceed without the user's confirmation. Record the chosen `fileId`.

## Step 2 — Load the defects

```bash
node .claude/skills/defects-update/scripts/read_defects.mjs rows <fileId>
```

Returns `{ header, rows, rowCount, uniqueKeyCount }`. Each row is an object keyed by column name:
`Key, Summary, Defect Classification, Component/s, Priority, Fix Version, Assignee, Status,
Labels/User Story, Description, Sprint, Date Created, Remarks`.

Report `rowCount` and `uniqueKeyCount`. The `Key` (e.g. `GEMPOS-266`) is the unique defect id and the
**dedup key**. If `rowCount != uniqueKeyCount`, list the duplicate Keys and ask how to handle them before continuing.

## Step 3 — Build the Story label index (source of truth)

```
searchJiraIssuesUsingJql(cloudId, jql="project = SCRUM AND issuetype = Story ORDER BY key ASC",
                         fields=["summary","labels","customfield_10020"], maxResults=100)
```

For each Story, for every label matching `GSMR-\d+`, map `label -> { storyKey, summary, sprintId }`, where
`sprintId` is the Story's current sprint — the `state=="active"` entry in `customfield_10020` if present,
otherwise the last (most recent) entry; `null` if the Story has no sprint. This sprint is what new Bugs inherit
in Step 7. If the query returns no stories, fall back to `jira-key-mapping.csv` (`snapshot_key` = GSMR label,
`jira_key` = Story) — in which case `sprintId` is unavailable and new Bugs are created without a sprint.

## Step 4 — Map each defect to a Story

For each row, extract the set of `GSMR-\d+` tokens found in **both** `Labels/User Story` and `Summary`
(note the spreadsheet uses suffixed forms like `GSMR-5_Income`; the regex still extracts `GSMR-5`). Dedupe the tokens:

- **Exactly one** token that exists in the Story index → **mapped** to that Story.
- **More than one distinct** known token → **ambiguous (overlap)**.
- **Zero** tokens, or only tokens absent from the Story index → **ambiguous (unknown)**.

Keep a running map `Key -> storyKey` for mapped rows, and a list of ambiguous rows with their candidate stories.

## Step 5 — Resolve ambiguous rows (one consolidated review)

Present **all** ambiguous rows together in a single table: `Key`, `Summary` (truncated), detected tokens, and
candidate stories. Ask the user to assign each ambiguous row to one Story key (or to **skip** it). Apply the
answers to the `Key -> storyKey` map. Do not prompt row-by-row.

## Step 6 — Verify mapping completeness (gate)

Before any Jira writes, assert that **every unique `Key`** is either mapped to a Story or explicitly skipped by
the user. Print: per-Story defect counts, the total mapped, and the skip list. If anything is unaccounted for,
return to Step 5. Only continue once this holds.

## Step 7 — Create new Bugs / update status of existing Bugs (idempotent; dedup on the Key label)

First, read Bug field metadata once to learn whether `priority` is on the Bug screen and its allowed values:
`getJiraIssueTypeMetaWithFields(cloudId, projectIdOrKey="SCRUM", issueTypeId="10007")`.

Process defects **in small batches** (e.g. 10–20). For each mapped defect:

1. **Find existing:** `searchJiraIssuesUsingJql(cloudId, jql='project = SCRUM AND issuetype = Bug AND labels = "<Key>"', fields=["summary","labels","status","issuelinks"])`.
2. **If NOT found → create:** summary, labels, description (with metadata block), priority, assignee, sprint, then transition and link.
3. **If found → check status change first:** no change = skip entirely; status change = update all fields + transition + ensure link.
4. **Status mapping:** Open/Reopened/Pending Information → To Do; In Progress/In Verification/Resolved/Pending Deployment → In Progress; Closed → Released.

Track counts: created, status-updated, unchanged, skipped (user-skipped), failed.

## Step 8 — Verify no duplicates and full coverage (gate)

Re-query all Bugs and assert every processed `Key` appears in exactly one Bug's labels. Report final summary:
total defects, created, status-updated, unchanged, skipped, failures, and any Key that is missing or duplicated.

---

## Notes
- The `atlassian` MCP currently points at the SSE endpoint being retired after 30 Jun 2026; update `.mcp.json` to the Streamable HTTP URL if Jira calls fail.
- `read_defects.mjs` is read-only against Drive (`drive.readonly`). It cannot write to the sheet.
- Re-running is safe: existing Bugs are never duplicated; only status is touched if the source sheet shows a change.
/dashboard-comms
Skill
Pulls all active-sprint Bugs from Jira, renders a self-contained interactive HTML dashboard (severity × status matrix, drill-down, multi-select filters), detects changes since the last refresh, and — when changes are found — drafts tailored stakeholder communications for Executive Leadership, Engineering, and the External Customer. The dashboard can be viewed at here.
---
name: dashboard-comms
description: Build a self-contained HTML dashboard of defects across all ACTIVE sprints in the Transformation Project (SCRUM) Jira — broken down by severity (from Priority) and status, with drill-down/filter by sprint and full summary+description per defect — plus a "changes since last refresh" panel. When a refresh detects changes, draft a status-update communication tailored to Executive Leadership, the Engineering Team, and the External Customer (each with an avatar and a copy button) embedded in the same HTML, prompting the user for non-Jira inputs and for review edits. Invoke with /dashboard-comms.
user-invocable: true
---

# Dashboard + Comms

Build an interactive HTML defect dashboard for the **active sprints** of the Transformation
Project, detect what changed since the last refresh, and — only when there are changes — draft
stakeholder-tailored status communications inside the same HTML. Follow the steps **in order** and
do not skip the gates (Step 3 decides whether comms are drafted; Step 5 is a human review loop).

## Fixed facts about this environment

- **Jira site / cloudId:** `vidhyaunni6.atlassian.net` → `3215a260-ee4c-42fd-8255-0c4dec1fb617`
- **Project "Transformation Project" = key `SCRUM`** (team-managed). Bug issuetype id `10007`.
- **Severity = Jira Priority:** `Highest→Critical, High→Major, Medium→Moderate, Low→Minor`.
- **Helper scripts:** `scripts/distill.mjs` (turns MCP search dumps into compact `defects.json`) and
  `scripts/build_dashboard.mjs` (renders the HTML and computes the refresh diff).

Working files live in `dashboard-comms/` under the project root: `defects.json` (current pull),
`snapshot.json` (previous pull, for diffing), `comms.json` (stakeholder content). The dashboard is
written to `defects-dashboard.html` in the project root, and timestamped PDF snapshots to
`dashboard-snapshots/`. **Run all node commands from the project root.**

---

## Step 1 — Pull active-sprint defects (Jira MCP) and distill to `defects.json`

Query open sprints' bugs via `searchJiraIssuesUsingJql` (paging until `isLast`), requesting only the
required fields. Hand the saved MCP result file(s) to the distiller:

```bash
node .claude/skills/dashboard-comms/scripts/distill.mjs <savedResultPage1.txt> [<page2.txt> ...]
```

`distill.mjs` writes `dashboard-comms/defects.json` (deduped by key) and prints
`{totalDefects, activeSprints, perSprint, perSeverity}`.

## Step 2 — Build the dashboard and compute the diff

```bash
node .claude/skills/dashboard-comms/scripts/build_dashboard.mjs
```

Writes `defects-dashboard.html` and prints a JSON diff summary:
`{ hasPrevSnapshot, hasChanges, counts:{new,closed,statusChanged,removed}, criticalMajorChanges:[...] }`.

## Step 3 — Decide whether to draft comms (gate)

- **No prior snapshot (first run):** baseline established — do not draft comms. Skip to Step 6.
- **Prior snapshot, no changes:** no new comms — previously approved comms persist. Skip to Step 6.
- **Prior snapshot + changes detected:** continue to Step 4.

## Step 4 — Gather non-Jira inputs, then draft tailored comms

Ask the user (single prompt) for mitigation/recovery plan and decisions/escalations needed.
Then draft three concise stakeholder summaries grounded in the diff data and sprint end-date risk:
- **Executive Leadership** — RAG health summary, open Critical count, timeline risk, one-line recommendation.
- **Engineering Team** — defect breakdown by component/story, blocked items, sprint action items.
- **External Customer** — plain-language confidence update, no jargon or defect counts.

Write `dashboard-comms/comms.json`, then re-render: `node .claude/skills/dashboard-comms/scripts/build_dashboard.mjs`

## Step 5 — Review loop (gate)

Present drafts per stakeholder, apply edits to `comms.json`, re-render. Repeat until approved.

## Step 6 — Finalize (persist snapshot + save PDF)

```bash
node .claude/skills/dashboard-comms/scripts/build_dashboard.mjs --finalize --pdf
```

Advances `snapshot.json` for the next diff and writes a timestamped PDF to `dashboard-snapshots/`
(labelled `_baseline` or `_update`). PDF requires headless Chrome; set `CHROME_PATH` if needed.

---

## Notes
- Re-running is safe and idempotent: `snapshot.json` only advances on `--finalize`.
- `comms.json` persists through no-change refreshes; only replaced when a change-bearing refresh drafts new comms.
- The dashboard HTML is fully self-contained — no network calls; safe to email or host on GitHub Pages.
- The `atlassian` MCP SSE endpoint is being retired after 30 Jun 2026; update `.mcp.json` to the Streamable HTTP URL if Jira calls fail.
Project Milestones
🔧
Tools & Setup
I established the foundational integrations — MCP servers, auth, and connectivity — before writing a single line of automation.
3 Milestones
🔌
Jira MCP Server Setup
How I connected Claude Code to Atlassian Jira
Completed
I established a live, authenticated connection between Claude Code and my Atlassian cloud instance (vidhyaunni6.atlassian.net) using the official Atlassian MCP server. This gave me the ability to read and write Jira issues, transitions, links, and metadata without ever leaving the terminal.
  • I configured .mcp.json with the Atlassian SSE MCP endpoint and my API credentials.
  • I verified MCP tool availability: createJiraIssue, editJiraIssue, searchJiraIssuesUsingJql, and 20+ others.
  • I confirmed project access to the Transformation Project (key: SCRUM, cloud ID: 3215a260-…).
  • I verified issue types: Bug (10007), Story (10004), and field metadata for each.
📋
JIRA User Stories from Snapshot
How I seeded the backlog from requirement screenshots
Completed
I analysed three requirement snapshot images and created 17 structured Jira User Stories in the SCRUM project, each labelled with a GSMR key so I could link bugs to stories downstream.
  • I extracted 17 requirements from the snapshot images using Claude's vision capabilities.
  • I created 17 Jira Stories via the MCP createJiraIssue tool.
  • I applied GSMR labels to each story as the traceability anchor for all downstream bug linking.
  • I validated the label map against live Jira and saved it in jira-key-mapping.csv as a static fallback.
☁️
Google Drive Auth Setup
OAuth2 credentials via MCP package · Sheet exported programmatically via googleapis
Completed
MCP Server Role
Authentication only — establishes and persists the OAuth2 refresh token. Does not read or export files.
Sheet Reading Role
read_defects.mjs — reuses the MCP's OAuth credentials to call the googleapis Drive API files.export endpoint and download the sheet as CSV.
🛠️
Why Claude Code?
Familiarity · Integration reliability · Toolchain fit
Rationale

I had working familiarity with both Claude Code and Gemini, and hold Pro subscriptions for both. The deciding factors were familiarity, integration reliability, and toolchain fit.

This project required stable integrations with Jira and Google Drive. I was aware that both Cursor and Claude Code support the Atlassian MCP server — giving me a direct, structured connection to Jira without custom API wiring. On the Google Drive side, my previous experience with Gemini had surfaced a consistent limitation: despite having the capability, it repeatedly failed to recognise or act on Google Drive integration without multiple manual nudges per session. That friction was a material risk for a project where Drive reads are a core workflow step.

Claude Code's MCP architecture offered predictable, configurable tool access from the outset — and combined with my existing familiarity with the environment, it was the pragmatic choice for a time-boxed assessment where toolchain reliability directly affected delivery.

🧭
Approach
I interpreted the requirements, structured them in Jira, and translated the defect process into a fully automated workflow.
2 Milestones
🤖
/defects-update Skill
How I automated UAT defect sync from a Google Sheet into Jira — read via googleapis, not the Drive MCP
Completed
I built a project-scoped Claude Code skill (/defects-update) that reads the UAT defect list from a Google Sheet via the googleapis Drive API (the MCP server provides OAuth2 credentials only — it cannot export Sheets directly) and creates or updates Jira Bugs idempotently — with full traceability, status mapping, and story linking. I encoded an 8-step workflow covering version discovery, RFC-4180 CSV parsing, story mapping, interactive ambiguity resolution, upsert logic, status transitions, issue linking, and final coverage verification. See the full skill breakdown below.

Note on data source abstraction: Google Drive is used here as the defect source for demonstration purposes, as it provides a readily accessible, OAuth2-authenticated file store within the MCP ecosystem. In a production enterprise context, the source adapter in read_defects.mjs can be pointed at any versioned document store that exposes a programmatic API — such as a corporate SharePoint/OneDrive tenant via Microsoft Graph, a Confluence attachment via the REST API, a network share exposed through an SFTP endpoint, or an ITSM system's native export feed. The skill's internal contract — structured row data with a defined column schema — is decoupled from the transport layer, making the data source pluggable without changes to the core workflow logic.

📊
/dashboard-comms Skill
How I built a live defect dashboard with stakeholder communications
Completed
I built a second Claude Code skill (/dashboard-comms) that pulls all active-sprint Bugs directly from Jira, distils them via a Node helper (distill.mjs), and renders a fully self-contained HTML dashboard with a severity × status matrix, multi-select sprint/severity/status chip filters, and inline defect drill-down with full description — no network dependency at runtime.

The skill detects what changed since the last refresh using a snapshot.json diff and — only when changes are detected — prompts me for mitigation context and escalation decisions before drafting three tailored stakeholder communications:
  • Executive Leadership — RAG-style programme health summary with timeline risk and escalation signals; no technical detail.
  • Engineering Team — component-level defect breakdown, blocked items with root cause context, and sprint-specific action items.
  • External Customer — plain-language confidence update on resolution progress and go-live impact; no defect counts or jargon.
Each communication renders as a card with an inline SVG avatar and a copy-to-clipboard button. The dashboard is written to defects-dashboard.html and a timestamped PDF snapshot is archived to dashboard-snapshots/ on each baseline and change-bearing refresh.
Key Prompts Used
🤖
/defects-update Skill
Approach · Phase 4
Skill definition
"Create a Claude Code skill called /defects-update that reads the UAT defect list from the Transformation Project folder in Google Drive and creates Jira bugs in the SCRUM project for each defect."
Story mapping logic
"Map each defect to its parent user story using the GSMR token in the 'Labels/User Story' column. If a defect matches exactly one story, auto-map it. If it matches none or multiple, flag it for me to resolve manually."
Idempotency requirement
"Make the bug creation idempotent — store the defect's GEMPOS key as a Jira label, search for it before writing, and update the existing bug rather than creating a duplicate if one already exists."
Ambiguity UX decision
"Don't ask me about ambiguous rows one at a time. Collect all of them into a single table, show them to me at once, and let me assign each one before any writes happen."
Traceability design
"For each bug, copy the GSMR label from its mapped story onto the bug and also create a 'Relates' issue link from the bug to the story — I want it searchable by label and visible as a link in Jira."
Scope constraint
"Restrict the skill to the Transformation Project Drive folder only and use V1.0 of the defect sheet — there is a V1.1 elsewhere in Drive but that is out of scope for this cycle."
Human review — version confirmation
"Before you load any rows, tell me the file name, version number, and last modified date of the sheet you're about to use, and wait for my confirmation before continuing."
Human review — ambiguous row assignment
"Show me all the defect rows you couldn't auto-map in a single table — key, summary, and what you detected — and wait for me to assign each one to a story before you write anything to Jira."
Human review — pre-write gate
"Before creating or updating any Jira bugs, show me the full mapping — how many defects mapped to each story, how many are being skipped, and the total count — and get my go-ahead before proceeding."
📊
/dashboard-comms Skill
Approach · Phase 5
Skill definition
"Build a /dashboard-comms skill that pulls all active-sprint Bugs from Jira and generates a self-contained HTML dashboard broken down by severity and status, with filters and drill-down."
Change detection
"Compare the current pull against a saved snapshot and surface what changed — new bugs, closed bugs, status changes — so the dashboard shows a 'changes since last refresh' panel."
Stakeholder communications
"When changes are detected, draft three separate status update communications — one for Executive Leadership, one for the Engineering Team, and one for the External Customer — each tailored in tone and depth. Embed them in the same dashboard HTML with a copy button per audience."
PDF snapshot archiving
"Save a timestamped PDF snapshot of the rendered dashboard on the baseline run and on every refresh that has changes, so I have a point-in-time audit trail."
Human input — mitigation context
"Before you draft any communications, ask me in a single prompt what the mitigation plan is for the critical and major defects that changed, and what decisions or escalations are needed from leadership, engineering, or the customer."
Human review — communications approval
"Show me all three draft communications — one per stakeholder — and wait for my edits and approval before finalising. Don't save the snapshot or generate the PDF until I've confirmed I'm happy with the content."
Skill Deep-Dive
⚡
/defects-update
I built this skill to sync my UAT defect list from Google Drive into Jira as Bugs — idempotently, with story linking and status mapping.
/defects-update

What I built this skill to do

1Version Discovery

I scan the "Transformation Project" Drive folder for all versions of the defect list, sorted newest-first, and confirm the correct version before proceeding.

2Defect Parsing

I export the Google Sheet as CSV and parse it with an RFC-4180–compliant parser that handles multi-line description fields — 141 rows, 141 unique GEMPOS-* keys, 13 columns.

3Story Mapping

I query live Jira for SCRUM Stories and build a GSMR-key → Story index, then extract the GSMR-\d+ token from each defect's "Labels/User Story" column to map it to exactly one Story.

4Ambiguity Resolution

I collect rows that match multiple stories or none (~16 rows in V1.0) into a single review table so I can manually assign them before any Jira writes occur.

5Idempotent Upsert

I store each defect's GEMPOS-* key as a Jira Bug label and search for it before writing — updating if it exists, creating if it doesn't — so re-running the skill never produces duplicates.

6Status Transition

After each create/update, I fetch available workflow transitions and map the source status: Open/Reopened → To Do; In Progress/In Verification → In Progress; Closed → Done.

7Story Linking

I create a "Relates" issue link from each Bug to its mapped Story (if not already present), giving me bidirectional traceability in Jira without duplicating links on re-runs.

8Coverage Verification

After all writes, I re-query Jira and assert every processed GEMPOS-* key appears in exactly one Bug — confirming full coverage and zero duplicates, then print a final created/updated/skipped/failed summary.

8-Step Execution Workflow

1
Locate latest version

I run read_defects.mjs versions to list Drive files matching my defect-list pattern, sorted newest-first, and confirm the version before continuing.

Google DriveUser Confirmation
2
Load defect rows

I run read_defects.mjs rows <fileId> to export the Sheet as CSV and parse all 141 rows with an RFC-4180 parser, safely handling multi-line cells.

Google DriveAutomated
3
Build Story label index

I query Jira (project = SCRUM AND issuetype = Story) and build a GSMR token → Story key map, falling back to jira-key-mapping.csv if the live query returns empty.

JiraAutomated
4
Map each defect to a Story

I extract GSMR-\d+ from the "Labels/User Story" column and Summary prefix. Exactly one match → auto-mapped; zero or multiple → flagged ambiguous.

Automated
5
Resolve ambiguous rows

I review a single table of all ~16 ambiguous rows, assigning each to a Story or skipping it. No Jira writes happen until I confirm this step.

User Confirmation
6
Verify mapping completeness

I assert every unique GEMPOS-* key is mapped or explicitly skipped, print per-story counts and the skip list, and only then allow writes to proceed.

AutomatedGate
7
Upsert Bugs with full attributes

I search for an existing Bug by GEMPOS-* label and create or update it — setting summary, description, priority, labels, assignee, workflow status, and a "Relates" link to the mapped Story.

JiraAutomated
8
Verify coverage & print summary

I re-query all SCRUM Bugs and assert each processed Key appears in exactly one Bug, then report my final created, updated, skipped, and failed counts.

JiraAutomated

Technical Architecture

📄 Google Sheet
UAT Defect List V1.0
→
🟢 read_defects.mjs
Node helper · OAuth2 auth
→
🤖 Claude (skill)
Reasoning · Mapping · Gate
→
🔵 Atlassian MCP
createJiraIssue · editJiraIssue…
→
📌 Jira SCRUM
Bugs + Links

I wrote read_defects.mjs to handle all Drive I/O — the gdrive MCP tool cannot export Sheets — using the googleapis library already in my node_modules, authenticated via my OAuth keys and stored refresh token. I kept all Jira writes in Claude's hands via the Atlassian MCP, so credentials and write logic stay cleanly separated from the Drive layer.

Iterations: How the Approach Evolved
  • Drive MCP → googleapis redirect. AI's first action was to call the gdrive MCP to export the sheet. It returned an invalid_request error — the MCP handles OAuth only and cannot export Google Sheets. Redirected AI to build read_defects.mjs, a custom Node script that reuses the MCP's OAuth credentials but calls the googleapis files.export endpoint directly.
  • Column format correction. AI built the GSMR token extraction assuming bare values like GSMR-5. The actual column used suffixed values — e.g. GSMR-5_Income. Caught after reviewing the real sheet data; the regex was corrected to strip the suffix before token lookup.
  • Ambiguity resolution redesign. AI's default was to handle each unmappable row inline as it went. Redesigned to a single consolidated review table — all ~16 ambiguous rows presented at once, one assignment pass, then proceed. No Jira writes until the full set is resolved.
  • Wrong Drive folder corrected. AI located a version of the defect sheet stored outside the designated "Transformation Project" folder. Corrected by providing the exact folder ID; now hardcoded in read_defects.mjs to prevent recurrence on future runs.
  • Priority field graceful degradation added. AI wrote the Priority mapping logic without first checking whether the field is exposed on team-managed Bug screens. Added a field metadata check before each write; if unavailable, the priority value is preserved in the Bug description instead.
📊
/dashboard-comms
I built this skill to turn live Jira data into an interactive defect dashboard and generate stakeholder-ready communications — automatically, on every refresh that detects a change.
/dashboard-comms
What it does

When invoked, the skill pulls all Bugs from every active sprint in the SCRUM project, distils them into a compact JSON, and renders a fully self-contained HTML dashboard. On subsequent runs it diffs against the previous snapshot — and only when changes are found does it prompt me for context and draft targeted communications.

6-Step Workflow
1
Pull active-sprint Bugs

Queries Jira via MCP (searchJiraIssuesUsingJql) for all Bugs in open sprints, requesting only the fields needed. Results are handed to distill.mjs which writes dashboard-comms/defects.json.

2
Build the dashboard & compute the diff

Runs build_dashboard.mjs, which renders defects-dashboard.html and outputs a JSON diff summary showing new, closed, status-changed, and removed defects since the last snapshot.

3
Gate: draft comms only on change

If no prior snapshot exists (baseline run) or no changes are detected, comms are not drafted — the dashboard notes "baseline established" or "no changes since last refresh". Any previously approved communications persist in the HTML automatically.

4
Gather non-Jira inputs & draft communications

Asks me in a single prompt for mitigation actions and escalation decisions, then drafts three audience-specific updates — Executive Leadership, Engineering Team, External Customer — grounded in the diff data and sprint end-date risk. Written to dashboard-comms/comms.json and re-embedded into the HTML.

5
Review loop

Presents all three draft communications for my review and edits per stakeholder. Repeats until I confirm approval. The HTML is re-rendered after each edit cycle.

6
Finalise — persist snapshot & archive PDF

Runs build_dashboard.mjs --finalize --pdf, advancing snapshot.json (so the next run can diff against it) and writing a timestamped PDF to dashboard-snapshots/ — labelled _baseline or _update accordingly.

Dashboard Features
  • Severity × Status matrix — Critical, Major, Moderate, Minor across To Do / In Progress / Done
  • Multi-select chip filters by Sprint, Severity, and Status — combinable, real-time
  • Inline defect drill-down with full Jira description; no browser tab required
  • "Changes since last refresh" panel — new, closed, and status-changed defects highlighted
  • Three stakeholder communication cards with inline SVG avatars and one-click copy buttons
  • Fully self-contained HTML — no external network calls; safe to email or share as a file
  • Timestamped PDF snapshot archive for baseline and each change-bearing refresh
Iterations: How the Approach Evolved
  • Comms gate introduced. AI's initial design drafted stakeholder communications on every invocation — including runs where nothing had changed. Introduced the snapshot diff gate: comms are only drafted when the diff detects actual changes; the last approved set persists through clean refreshes automatically.
  • MCP payload handling redirected. The raw Jira MCP response for active-sprint Bugs was approximately 220K characters — too large to parse inline reliably. AI attempted inline parsing on the first run. Redirected to save the MCP result to file and pass it to distill.mjs, which produces a compact, structured defects.json for all downstream processing.
  • Dashboard layout evolved. The initial output was a flat list of defect records. Evolved to a severity × status matrix with multi-select sprint, severity, and status chip filters — giving stakeholders an at-a-glance health view alongside the ability to drill into individual defects without leaving the page.
🔍
Critical Assessment
Risks, limitations, and constraints I identified during the project — along with what I'd do differently and where I had to step in.
Key Findings
🤔
What did AI get wrong or miss? Where did you have to correct it?
Honest reflection on the gaps — across both skills

Across both skills, AI made a recurring pattern of assumptions — about tool capabilities, data formats, and operational context — that I had to catch, diagnose, and correct. The mistakes fell into two distinct categories: technical failures in how AI approached the implementation, and design gaps where AI's output was functional but wrong for the context.

/defects-update — Mistakes & Corrections
  • AI assumed the Drive MCP could read the sheet — it cannot. AI's first action was to call the gdrive MCP tool to export the defect sheet. It returned an invalid_request error because the MCP server handles OAuth2 authentication only and cannot export Google Sheets. AI had not verified this constraint before designing around it. I diagnosed the failure, identified the correct approach, and redirected AI to build read_defects.mjs — a custom Node script that reuses the MCP's OAuth credentials but calls the googleapis files.export endpoint directly.
  • AI missed an OAuth token refresh failure during auth setup. The server-gdrive package was silently allowing tokens to expire between sessions. AI did not detect this until a Drive read failed mid-workflow. I identified the root cause and instructed AI to replace the package's default auth with an explicit OAuth2 + setCredentials construction to ensure tokens refresh reliably — a check that should have been part of the initial setup validation.
  • AI picked up the defect sheet from the wrong Drive folder. When searching for the UAT defect list, AI located a version stored outside the designated "Transformation Project" folder. It had not applied the scope constraint I specified. I intervened to provide the exact folder ID and enforce the rule — which is now hardcoded in read_defects.mjs to prevent recurrence.
  • AI misread the "Labels/User Story" column format, masking a data quality problem. AI assumed the column would contain bare GSMR tokens like GSMR-5, but the actual values were suffixed — e.g. GSMR-5_Income. I corrected the regex. Once fixed, the mapping exposed a further issue AI had not flagged: approximately 16 of 72 defect rows could not be auto-mapped — 2 had overlapping tokens pointing to multiple stories, and 14 had no recognisable token at all. The successfully mapped defects were distributed across six GSMR stories, with the remaining ~16 requiring manual assignment in a consolidated review before any Jira writes proceeded.
  • AI assumed the Priority field was always writable in Jira. It built the priority-mapping logic without first verifying whether the field is exposed on team-managed Bug screens — which it frequently is not. I caught this gap and pushed AI to add a graceful-degradation path that checks field metadata before attempting to write priority, preserving the value in the Bug description if the field is unavailable.
/dashboard-comms — Mistakes & Corrections
  • AI generated stakeholder communications on every refresh, regardless of whether anything changed. AI's initial design treated every invocation as a trigger for drafting new comms — including runs where the defect data was identical to the previous snapshot. This would have produced redundant, potentially confusing communications to stakeholders on no-change days. I introduced the snapshot diff gate: comms are drafted only when changes are detected, and the last approved set is preserved through clean refreshes.
  • AI underestimated the size of the Jira MCP response and tried to parse it inline. When querying active-sprint Bugs, the raw MCP payload was approximately 220K characters — far too large to process reliably within the conversation context. AI's initial approach attempted inline parsing, which was fragile and impractical at scale. I redirected it to save the MCP result to file and pass it to distill.mjs for structured processing, which is both more robust and repeatable.
🧠
What did you add that AI couldn't? Your judgment, context, expertise?
Where human decision-making was irreplaceable — across both skills

Across both skills, the things that made this project actually work — not just technically function — came from me. AI executed; I directed, bounded, and made the calls that required programme context.

/defects-update
  • Defining scope before the skill could run. I enforced that the source must be strictly the "Transformation Project" folder and strictly V1.0. V1.1 existed in Drive and AI would have flagged it as the latest version — only I knew it was out of scope for this cycle. That constraint is now hardcoded, but it originated from my operational knowledge of the project.
  • The traceability architecture. I decided to copy the GSMR label onto each Bug and create a "Relates" link to the parent story — label for JQL searchability, link for visual traceability in the Jira board. AI presented multiple options; I made the call based on how the team actually navigates issues during a sprint.
  • The ambiguity resolution UX. When ~16 defects could not be auto-mapped, I directed AI to present all of them in a single consolidated table — key, summary, detected tokens — and wait for one assignment pass before writing anything to Jira. AI's default was to handle each row inline as it went. The consolidated-gate approach reflects how I'd manage a UAT review: resolve all edge cases first, then execute.
  • Keeping Jira writes human-supervised. I deliberately split the architecture — Node script for Drive reads, Claude + MCP for all Jira writes — so every write operation remained visible, reviewable, and interruptible at each step. A fully automated pipeline was technically achievable; I judged it inappropriate for an audit-sensitive defect sync.
/dashboard-comms
  • The communications gate rule. I defined that comms should only be drafted when the diff detects actual changes — never on a clean refresh. This reflects how status updates work in a programme context: you communicate change, not the absence of it. AI had no way to derive this from the data; it defaulted to generating an update on every run until I corrected the design.
  • Audience calibration across three stakeholder groups. I specified what each audience needed and, equally importantly, what to withhold. Executive Leadership gets risk and decision asks — not technical detail. Engineering gets specifics — defect keys, status transitions, sprint risk, and unblocking actions. The External Customer gets impact and timeline framing. AI can draft fluently once those parameters exist; defining them required my knowledge of how each group operates and what they're accountable for.
  • Knowing when the output was good enough to ship. I stopped the refinement loop at the right point. More subcommands, richer error handling, additional stakeholder tiers — all were technically possible, and AI proposed several. I made the call that the prototype was complete and further complexity would not add value for this assessment.
Project Reflection
01
Where was AI most helpful in this task?

AI was most valuable in the parts of both skills that were structured, high-volume, and execution-heavy — precisely where a TPM typically loses the most time. The prototype setup steps (user story creation, MCP wiring) were a useful foundation, but the real return came from the two skills built on top of them.

/defects-update — The core value was translating a plain-language workflow description into a reliable, idempotent 8-step sync. When I described what I needed — read the sheet, extract GSMR tokens, map each defect to its story, upsert Bugs with no duplicates, link them back — AI structured this into a repeatable process, wrote the read_defects.mjs helper, handled the RFC-4180 CSV parsing for multi-line description fields, and built the status transition logic from the Jira metadata it queried at runtime. What would otherwise require manual triage of 72 defect rows across 6 user stories, followed by individual Jira ticket creation, was reduced to a single invocation followed by one consolidated review pass for the ~16 ambiguous rows. I was operating as a decision-maker and reviewer throughout — not an implementer.

/dashboard-comms — AI's most tangible contribution here was eliminating the manual aggregation and formatting work that typically sits between a Jira query and a stakeholder update. AI pulled and distilled the active-sprint Bug data, rendered a fully self-contained interactive HTML dashboard with severity-status breakdowns and per-sprint drill-down, computed the diff against the previous snapshot, and drafted three tailored stakeholder communications in a single workflow. In a real programme context, this is the work that often takes a TPM the better part of a day — collating Jira data, building a status slide, and writing separate updates for leadership, engineering, and the customer. The skill compresses that to minutes, with the communications gate ensuring stakeholders only receive updates when there is something material to report.

Across both skills, AI also kept the implementation honest. Articulating requirements as structured prompts forced me to think through the design — confirmation gates, idempotency rules, the diff logic — before any code was written, rather than discovering gaps mid-build. The right framing is not AI replacing the TPM function, but AI amplifying it: taking on the mechanical execution weight so the practitioner can focus on the decisions that require experience, context, and accountability.

02
Where was it least helpful or even counterproductive?

AI struggled most when operational context wasn't explicitly stated — and across both skills, that gap produced real friction that I had to diagnose and correct.

In /defects-update, the most counterproductive moment was AI attempting to read the Google Sheet via the Drive MCP tool directly. It failed with an invalid_request error and didn't surface a clear alternative. I had to identify the root cause — the MCP handles OAuth only and cannot export Sheets — and redirect AI to build read_defects.mjs using the googleapis API. Beyond that, AI assumed the "Labels/User Story" column contained bare GSMR tokens and built the extraction logic around that assumption. The column was suffixed, the regex was wrong, and I had to catch it. Without programme knowledge of the data format, AI had no way to know — but the cost of the assumption was a full rework of the mapping logic mid-build.

In /dashboard-comms, the least helpful behaviour was AI's default to draft stakeholder communications on every invocation. It had no concept of whether anything had changed — it simply generated output because it was asked to run. I had to introduce the snapshot diff gate, making comms conditional on detected changes. Left uncorrected, the skill would have sent redundant updates to stakeholders on every clean refresh, which would have eroded trust in the communications quickly.

Across both skills, AI also had a consistent tendency to propose additions beyond the brief once the core was working — a check-auth subcommand for /defects-update, a --force-comms flag for /dashboard-comms. Both were reasonable enhancements; neither was required. This is where AI's instinct to be thorough works against a TPM's need to ship. Stopping the loop required a deliberate call on my part each time.

03
What would you do differently next time?

For /defects-update, I'd write a data contract before any implementation starts — a short document specifying the exact column format (including the GSMR-5_Income suffix pattern), which Drive folder is in scope, which sheet version is current, and which Jira fields are writable on team-managed Bug screens. Every assumption AI got wrong in this skill — the column format, the folder scope, the Priority field availability — was a constraint that existed in my head but was never handed to AI explicitly. A structured brief upfront would have eliminated those rework cycles. I'd also include sprint inheritance as an explicit requirement from the start: the initial implementation created Bugs without assigning them to the same sprint as their mapped user story, meaning they did not appear in the active sprint board view. This was corrected by querying the story's current sprint at mapping time and passing the sprint ID during Bug creation — but it should have been a stated requirement at the outset, not a gap discovered after the first sync. I'd also validate tool capabilities before designing around them: a five-minute check that the Drive MCP can actually export a Sheet would have shaped the architecture from the start rather than mid-build.

For /dashboard-comms, I'd define the comms gate rule and the diff behaviour in the initial prompt rather than correcting AI's default mid-session. The snapshot diff logic — what constitutes a change, when comms are triggered, how the baseline is established — is the core design decision of the skill. Specifying it upfront as a constraint, not discovering it as a correction, would have produced a cleaner first pass and saved a full redesign of the refresh flow.

Across both skills, I'd set an explicit "done" definition before starting each build. AI's instinct is to keep refining, and without a clear stopping point stated in the prompt, every working skill becomes a starting point for the next enhancement. Specifying the exact scope of done — which steps are in, which edge cases are out, what the prototype does not need to handle — keeps AI executing within the brief rather than optimising beyond it.

04
How might you apply this AI process to other TPM work?

The two skills built here are directly reusable patterns. The underlying model — connect AI to live systems via MCP, describe a structured workflow, gate execution on human confirmation, and produce versioned output — applies across a wide range of TPM work beyond this project.

1 Defect Sync Across Any UAT Cycle

The /defects-update skill is not Transformation Project-specific. Any UAT cycle producing a structured defect register — in a Google Sheet, SharePoint list, Confluence table, or internal CSV export — could feed the same idempotent sync into Jira. The GSMR label mapping would be replaced with the relevant traceability field for that programme; the 8-step workflow, ambiguity gate, and dedup logic carry over unchanged.

2 Standing Sprint Defect Reporting

The /dashboard-comms skill solves a recurring TPM problem: generating a consistent, stakeholder-appropriate status update from live Jira data without manual aggregation. Invoked on a weekly cadence or before each steering committee, it pulls the active sprint's Bugs, computes what changed since the last refresh, and drafts tailored updates for leadership, engineering, and external stakeholders. The comms gate ensures nothing is sent on a no-change week, preserving audience trust.

3 Release Readiness Scoring

An extension of /dashboard-comms: instead of a defect status update, the skill produces a go/no-go readiness scorecard — aggregating open Critical and Major bugs against the sprint end date, checking for unresolved story dependencies, and surfacing a risk rating for the release window. This replaces the manual Jira view-collation a TPM does before each release call.

4 Cross-Programme Defect Pattern Analysis

The distillation layer in /dashboard-comms — pulling structured Bug data and compressing it into a queryable JSON — could be extended to surface patterns across multiple programmes: recurring defect components, high-severity backlogs by assignee, or stories with disproportionate defect density. This turns the dashboard from a status tool into a programme health early-warning system.

5 Requirement Ingestion to Jira Backlog

The MCP-to-Jira write pattern from /defects-update applies equally to backlog creation: ingest a BRD, PRD, or Confluence requirements page, extract structured stories with acceptance criteria, and upsert them idempotently into Jira. Re-running after a requirements revision updates only changed stories — the same idempotency logic, a different source format.

6 Stakeholder Comms for Any Programme Event

The comms drafting layer in /dashboard-comms is not limited to defect reporting. The same pattern — three audience-tailored updates grounded in live Jira data — applies to any programme milestone where story completions, dependency resolutions, or sprint outcomes need to be communicated to leadership, engineering, and external parties. The audience calibration in comms-guidance.md carries across contexts with minimal adjustment.

Artifacts
Skill File
/defects-update
8-step idempotent UAT defect sync from Google Drive into Jira as Bugs, with story mapping and status transitions.
View SKILL.md →
Skill File
/dashboard-comms
6-step interactive defect dashboard with stakeholder-tailored communications, snapshot diffing, and PDF archiving.
View SKILL.md → View Dashboard →

This assessment was completed entirely using Claude Code — Anthropic's agentic CLI — as the primary development environment. Every component of the solution, from the MCP-connected skill files and Node.js helper scripts to this documentation page itself, was built through iterative, prompt-driven collaboration with AI. I used it as an execution layer: building workflows, directing the implementation at each decision gate, and maintaining ownership of scope, data quality, and stakeholder context throughout. The professional artefacts produced — the interactive defect dashboard, the stakeholder communication drafts, the SKILL.md files, and this documentation — were generated as deliberate outputs of that process, not as post-hoc additions. The assessment reflects both what AI can execute when given clear direction, and the programme management judgement required to direct it effectively.
